## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

The race to build artificial intelligence infrastructure is the defining capital allocation event of the 2020s.
Between 2023 and 2025, the leading AI laboratories (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and their cloud infrastructure providers) committed over \$200 billion in 2024 alone, with combined capital expenditure from the four largest investors projected to reach \$660--690 billion in 2026 [@jassy2026confidence].
These investments are largely irreversible: a \$10 billion training cluster cannot be easily repurposed, and the competitive dynamics of AI development make delay potentially fatal.
Yet the fundamental uncertainty driving these decisions---whether and when AI capabilities will reach a transformative threshold---remains profoundly unresolved.

A frontier AI laboratory produces intelligence by combining two forms of compute. *Training compute* is used to optimize a neural network's parameters on massive datasets, yielding a model whose quality scales as a power law of compute invested [@kaplan2020scaling; @altman2025observations]. *Inference compute* runs the trained model to serve predictions and generate revenue. Both activities consume the same scarce hardware, purpose-built GPU clusters housed in multi-billion-dollar data centers, creating an intertemporal allocation problem with no close parallel in traditional capital budgeting.^[In practice, AI infrastructure investment also encompasses substantial expenditure on power generation and transmission---dedicated natural gas plants, long-term power purchase agreements, on-site substations---required to supply the hundreds of megawatts that a single training cluster demands. For tractability, I model all of these complementary investments as a single capital outlay in "compute capacity," with the cost function $I(K) = cK^\gamma$ absorbing the full cost of bringing a unit of capacity online, including its power supply.] The infrastructure is highly irreversible: facilities take 18--24 months to build, GPU architectures depreciate on similar cycles, and algorithmic breakthroughs can devalue installed capacity overnight. Executives frame the resulting risk calculus in starkly asymmetric terms. Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai has argued that "the risk of underinvesting is dramatically greater than the risk of overinvesting" [@pichai2024risk], while Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang contends that agentic AI and reasoning require "easily 100x more" compute than anticipated just one year prior [@huang2025scaling]. The prize these investments target, what this paper models as a regime switch to high demand, is variously called "transformative AI" or "Artificial General Intelligence" (AGI): a step change in model capability sufficient to automate a broad swath of cognitive tasks and generate sustained, qualitatively higher demand for compute [@amodei2024machines]. Executive timeline estimates for this event range from "superintelligence in a few thousand days" [@altman2024intelligence] and 2026--2027 [@amodei2024machines] to "not with current architectures" [@lecun2025autoregressive], and it is precisely this dispersion in beliefs that drives the heterogeneity in investment behavior that the model is designed to explain.

This paper develops a unified model of irreversible capacity investment under demand uncertainty that captures the distinctive features of AI infrastructure competition.
I combine real options theory [@mcdonald1986value; @dixit1994investment] with regime switching [@guo2005investment], duopoly competition [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic], endogenous default risk [@leland1994corporate], and diminishing returns calibrated to empirical scaling laws [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training].
The primary contribution is the integrated framework itself: placing a training-inference allocation at the center of a regime-switching investment option with strategic competition and endogenous default.
This combination generates mechanisms that no subset of the building blocks can produce on its own.
The results fall into three categories.

First, I provide analytical and semi-analytical characterization of the optimal investment policy.
Firms choose not only when to invest and how much capacity to install, but how to split that capacity between model training (which builds capabilities for the future) and inference serving (which generates revenue today).
In the current (low) regime, revenue depends on inference capacity through a Tullock contest; in the post-adoption (high) regime, revenue depends on training quality.
This regime-specific competition creates a genuine strategic trade-off absent from standard capacity models: a firm that allocates too much to training sacrifices current revenue and risks default, while a firm that allocates too little sacrifices its competitive position in the transformative AI regime.
Under exogenous hazard, I provide analytical characterization of the optimal training fraction, investment trigger, and capacity (Proposition 1); comparative statics under endogenous hazard are established numerically.
Duopoly preemption changes both timing and training incentives: the leader allocates more to training than the follower because the monopoly phase eliminates the opportunity cost of forgone inference revenue (Proposition 3).

Second, the interaction of training allocation with leverage and default risk produces a *faith-based survival* mechanism: training investment raises the effective revenue coefficient $A_{\text{eff}}$ through the expected post-switch continuation value, lowering the default boundary (Proposition 2).
Within the model, training investment sustains solvency by raising the expected post-switch continuation value, but only in parameter regions where this channel dominates current cash-flow weakness---a condition I characterize in closed form for the symmetric duopoly (@eq-phi-underbar).

Third, I introduce the *AI investment dilemma*: the cost to a firm of investing according to a $\lambda$ that differs from its true belief. The asymmetry of this trade-off is captured by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei's observation: "I could buy \$1 trillion of compute that starts at the end of 2027. If my revenue is not \$1 trillion dollars, if it's even \$800 billion, there's no force on Earth, there's no hedge on Earth that could stop me from going bankrupt" [@amodei2026cone].
If a firm is conservative (investing with $\lambda_{\text{invest}} < \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it invests too late and loses first-mover advantage; if aggressive ($\lambda_{\text{invest}} > \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it overbuilds and risks default.
The training channel amplifies this asymmetry: an overly optimistic firm over-allocates to training, sacrificing the inference revenue it needs to survive.
A stylized calibration to four AI lab archetypes illustrates that the asymmetry is substantial: aggressive overinvestment carries higher downside risk than conservative underinvestment, and the observed capital commitments are consistent with genuinely optimistic beliefs about AI timelines.

The model focuses on duopoly as the core strategic setting.
This is a deliberate scope choice, not a limitation.
Frontier AI training competition is highly concentrated: as of 2025, the vast majority of the world's largest training runs are conducted by a handful of laboratories, often backed by or partnered with a single cloud provider (e.g., Anthropic--Amazon, OpenAI--Microsoft).
The dominant strategic margin is not atomistic competition but rivalry among a few labs, each making multi-billion-dollar capacity commitments.
Duopoly is the minimal model that captures the key forces: strategic preemption, race-like training allocation incentives, and continuation-value effects under leverage.
The paper does not claim the industry is literally two firms; it claims that duopoly captures the dominant strategic force.
Broader oligopoly crowding, coalition effects, entry and exit of smaller labs, and endogenous financing interactions across many firms are important but second-order relative to the mechanisms studied here.
I verify numerically that the qualitative patterns survive with $N > 2$ firms in Appendix C.

{{< include _literature.qmd >}}

The paper proceeds as follows.
@sec-model presents the model environment with training-inference allocation, endogenous regime switching, the single-firm benchmark, and the duopoly equilibrium with default risk; all analytical results are contained in this section.
@sec-calibration describes the stylized calibration to AI infrastructure firm archetypes and presents baseline numerical results.
@sec-valuation develops quantitative implications: growth option decomposition, credit risk, the AI investment dilemma, and equity valuation sensitivity.
@sec-discussion considers policy implications, testable predictions, and limitations.
@sec-conclusion concludes.
Supplementary material on the $N$-firm numerical extension appears in Appendix C.
